behaviors.description: The speech-to-text output preserves speaker labels so that the summary can attribute statements to specific attendees.
behaviors.justification: Failure depends on the real speech-to-text API's response format and how the code parses speaker diarization. A mock API that assumes the correct format will always pass, hiding real incompatibilities. The LLM doesn't affect label preservation—only the speech-to-text API does. Real API call is necessary to catch format mismatches or parse errors.
behaviors.test_description: Send a real or near-real meeting audio file to the actual speech-to-text API, verify the output contains speaker labels in the expected structure, and confirm the parsing code correctly extracts speaker identities. Test should include edge cases like overlapping speech or unclear speaker boundaries.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Speaker label preservation is purely a data-format concern between the speech-to-text API and the parser—no LLM intelligence or multi-step orchestration needed. System level is both necessary and sufficient.

behaviors.description: The structured summary contains at least one action item for every attendee who was assigned a task during the meeting.
behaviors.justification: Correctness depends entirely on what the real LLM produces. A mock LLM that always returns perfect action items will pass even if the real LLM misses assignments, confuses attendees, or fails to extract all owner-task pairs. This is non-deterministic (different runs produce different extractions) and requires the LLM's intelligence to identify assignments correctly. No amount of mocking hides the real failure mode: 'the LLM missed or misattributed an action item'.
behaviors.test_description: Create test meeting transcripts with known attendees and explicit action items assigned to them (e.g., 'Alice will handle the report by Friday'). Call the real LLM API multiple times and verify that the returned action items include all assignments. Capture failures where the LLM missed attendees or action items that were clearly stated in the transcript.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: This behavior is purely about LLM extraction correctness. No real speech-to-text API or multi-step orchestration is needed—just the LLM. Agentic level is the right minimum.

behaviors.description: Key decisions mentioned in the transcript appear in the 'decisions' section of the summary, not buried in the narrative.
behaviors.justification: The LLM must understand what constitutes a 'decision', extract it, and place it in the correct output field. A mock LLM that always returns properly structured output will pass even if the real LLM misclassifies information or puts decisions in the wrong field. Like behavior 2, this is non-deterministic—the LLM may categorize things differently across runs. Mocking hides the real failure: the LLM failed to identify or correctly categorize a decision.
behaviors.test_description: Prepare test transcripts with clear decision statements (e.g., 'We've decided to migrate to Postgres'). Call the real LLM and verify decisions appear in the 'decisions' field, not in the narrative. Test edge cases: ambiguous statements, implicit decisions, decisions stated by different attendees. Run multiple times to catch non-determinism.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Like behavior 2, this is a pure LLM intelligence test—no API format concerns or orchestration involved. Agentic level is the right minimum.

behaviors.description: When the audio file is longer than 60 minutes, the service splits it into segments and processes them sequentially, merging the results into a single coherent summary.
behaviors.justification: This behavior requires real speech-to-text API calls (System-level) and real LLM calls (Agentic-level minimum). The key failure modes are: dropped content between segments, context loss during merging, or incoherence in the final summary. Whether merging is simple (concatenate transcripts → one LLM summary) or complex (segment-by-segment LLM summaries → merge with LLM) depends on implementation. Assuming the likely case of a single LLM call on concatenated transcripts, Agentic level is sufficient. If implementation uses per-segment summarization plus an additional merge LLM call, Workflow would be needed. Testing requires real speech-to-text and real LLM to ensure segments are processed and combined correctly.
behaviors.test_description: Create a real or synthetic >60 minute audio file (or use a real meeting recording). Verify the system splits it correctly (e.g., at 60-minute boundaries). Confirm all segments are transcribed and summarized. Check the final summary includes information from all segments (no dropped content). Verify the summary is coherent across segments, not just concatenated. Compare against ground truth to detect missing information or incoherence.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: This behavior likely requires Agentic level (real speech-to-text + single final LLM summary). If implementation uses multiple LLM calls per segment plus a merge step, Workflow would be the true minimum. Assigning probabilities to reflect both possibilities.

behaviors.description: The full pipeline — upload audio, transcribe, summarize, deliver structured output — completes without manual intervention for a real meeting recording.
behaviors.justification: This behavior tests the entire end-to-end pipeline as a coordinated workflow. It requires real speech-to-text API calls (System) and real LLM API calls (Agentic) orchestrated in sequence. Failure modes span the entire pipeline: API format mismatches, LLM output malformation, error handling gaps, timeouts, or orchestration errors (e.g., passing wrong context to the next step). A mocked version hides all of these. The test must exercise the real pipeline with real dependencies to ensure all steps integrate correctly and failures are handled gracefully.
behaviors.test_description: Execute the full pipeline with a real meeting recording (or high-fidelity synthetic audio). Verify each step completes: audio is uploaded, transcription succeeds with speaker labels intact, LLM summarization succeeds, output is parsed and structured correctly, and the final summary is delivered. Test with real-world edge cases: poor audio quality, long meetings (triggers segmentation), technical jargon, multiple speakers. Verify no step requires manual intervention or falls through error handling.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: This is explicitly a full pipeline test with real external APIs and multiple agentic steps. Workflow level is the correct minimum because the test must verify the entire orchestration works end-to-end, not just individual components.
